intro.speaker: Caller
intro.text: Wildlife biologist, Vern. Thirty years in the field. I found tracks last week that don't match any known species.

development.speaker: Vern
development.text: Tracks. Did you document them?

development.speaker: Caller
development.text: Photographed, measured, cast in plaster. Bipedal, eighteen inches long, five toes.

development.speaker: Vern
development.text: Eighteen inches. That's massive.

development.speaker: Caller
development.text: The stride length suggests something eight to nine feet tall. And there's dermal ridges in the casts.

Skeptical.speaker: Caller
Skeptical.text: Dermal ridges can't be faked easily. And the depth suggests significant weight.

Believing.speaker: Vern
Believing.text: Dermal ridges. That's the detail that separates real evidence from hoaxes.

Believing.speaker: Caller
Believing.text: Exactly. I've studied animal tracks my whole career. These are authentic.

conclusion.speaker: Vern
conclusion.text: Send us the documentation. This is important.

conclusion.speaker: Caller
conclusion.text: Already preparing a paper. Thanks, Vern.

intro.speaker: Caller
intro.text: Primatologist. PhD from Stanford. I found something in the Olympic Peninsula that changed my perspective.

development.speaker: Vern
development.text: What'd you find?

development.speaker: Caller
development.text: Hair samples. Analyzed them at three different labs. Primate DNA, but doesn't match any known species.

development.speaker: Vern
development.text: Unknown primate DNA.

development.speaker: Caller
development.text: The labs were baffled. It's closest to human and great ape, but distinct from both.

Skeptical.speaker: Caller
Skeptical.text: Three independent labs. Same results. Chain of custody was impeccable.

Believing.speaker: Vern
Believing.text: Three labs, same conclusion. That's compelling.

Believing.speaker: Caller
Believing.text: I was a skeptic too. The data changed my mind.

conclusion.speaker: Vern
conclusion.text: Thank you for sharing. Science matters.

intro.speaker: Vern
intro.text: {callerName} from {callerLocation}, I understand you have scientific credentials?

intro.speaker: Caller
intro.text: Anthropologist specializing in primate evolution, Vern. I've been quietly researching Sasquatch evidence for five years.

development.speaker: Vern
development.text: Quietly researching. Why quietly?

development.speaker: Caller
development.text: Career suicide to study this openly. But the evidence kept accumulating. Consistent footprint morphology across decades and regions.

development.speaker: Vern
development.text: What convinced you it warranted serious study?

development.speaker: Caller
development.text: The Patterson-Gimlin film. Modern biomechanical analysis shows the gait is impossible to fake with 1960s technology.

Skeptical.speaker: Vern
Skeptical.text: The Patterson film has been contested for decades.

Skeptical.speaker: Caller
Skeptical.text: Contested by people who haven't done the analysis. The muscle movement alone rules out a suit.

Believing.speaker: Vern
Believing.text: Biomechanical analysis is hard science. That carries weight.

Believing.speaker: Caller
Believing.text: The academic community is starting to pay attention. Slowly.

conclusion.speaker: Vern
conclusion.text: Thank you for your work. We need more rigorous researchers in this field.

conclusion.speaker: Caller
conclusion.text: The evidence deserves proper scientific inquiry. Thanks, Vern.

intro.speaker: Caller
intro.text: Genetics researcher, Vern. What started as debunking turned into something else entirely.

development.speaker: Vern
development.text: You tried to debunk and failed?

development.speaker: Caller
development.text: I analyzed tissue samples from multiple sources. Expected to find bear or human. Found neither.

development.speaker: Vern
development.text: What did you find?

development.speaker: Caller
development.text: Novel primate sequences. Mitochondrial DNA suggesting a divergence from human lineage about 15,000 years ago.

Skeptical.speaker: Caller
Skeptical.text: Submitted twice. Rejected both times without review. The topic is career poison.

Believing.speaker: Vern
Believing.text: Fifteen thousand years. That's within human migration timelines.

Believing.speaker: Caller
Believing.text: A parallel hominid species. It would rewrite human history.

conclusion.speaker: Vern
conclusion.text: Keep pushing for publication. The data will speak eventually.

conclusion.speaker: Caller
conclusion.text: That's the hope. Thanks for letting me share this, Vern.

intro.speaker: Vern
intro.text: {callerName} from {callerLocation}! A scientist calling in! Let's hear it!

intro.speaker: Caller
intro.text: Thermal imaging specialist, Vern! I captured something incredible in the Cascade Range!

development.speaker: Vern
development.text: Thermal footage! What did you get?!

development.speaker: Caller
development.text: Bipedal figure, nine feet tall! Heat signature consistent with a large mammal! Moving through dense forest at night!

development.speaker: Vern
development.text: Did you track it?!

development.speaker: Caller
development.text: For twenty minutes! It moved faster than any human could through that terrain! The footage is crystal clear!

Skeptical.speaker: Vern
Skeptical.text: Could it have been a person in a suit?!

Skeptical.speaker: Caller
Skeptical.text: Not at those temperatures! A suit would show different heat distribution! This was biological!

Believing.speaker: Vern
Believing.text: Twenty minutes of thermal footage! This could be definitive proof!

Believing.speaker: Caller
Believing.text: I'm having it professionally analyzed! This is real, Vern!

conclusion.speaker: Vern
conclusion.text: Send us that footage! The world needs to see this!

conclusion.speaker: Caller
conclusion.text: As soon as the analysis is done! Thank you, Vern!
